text: The surgeon attempts to open the retroperitoneum, but the loops of bowel adherent to it are not clearly visible. Upon approaching the fat, a greenish biliary appearance is discovered, prompting further investigation. Additional dissection reveals an inadvertent injury to the small bowel.

The surgeon uses scissors to make sharp cuts, freeing the bowel from retroperitoneal attachments and mobilizing it to identify the proximal and distal loops, aiming to localize the exact site of the bowel injury.

The surgeon uses cold knife scissors to dissect the bowel, revealing an injury caused by the harmonic device. The freed loop of bowel allows identification of the appendix in the lower frame. The injury is located approximately three to four centimeters proximal to the ileocecal junction on the ileum.

The surgeon closes the bowel injury in two layers, starting with the mucosal layer using Vicryl sutures in a continuous interlocking pattern. The loops are approximated to prevent stricturing, with careful mobilization of both distal and proximal segments to ensure correct suture placement direction.

The surgeon approximates the mucosal layer of the small bowel injury using absorbable suture material in a continuous manner. Following this, the second layer is closed with 3-0 silk on a round body needle in an interrupted fashion to reduce tension and reinforce the mucosal layer.
After approximating the second layer of sutures, the surgeon ties a patch of omentum over the suture line to reinforce the closure of the small bowel injury.

The surgeon completes the closure of the second layer of the bowel repair using sutures. A patch of omentum is visibly placed over the suture line to reinforce the closure, showing the final appearance of the repaired area.
